ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'beef burger' class (FOODON_00002764); no specific 'black pepper beef burger' subclass exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Hamburger / beef burger — bread-based meat dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers hamburger/beef burger category; black pepper variant not separately coded. Closest parent match documented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for beef burger with black pepper found in published SNOMED CT release; null used rather than fabricate a code.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard beef burger with black pepper sauce, approximately 180 g, comprising a beef patty, bun, black pepper sauce, and salad garnish.

nutrient_source.title: Beef Burger With Black Pepper — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Beef Burger With Black Pepper' (record G00010085). All per-100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is not separately reported by HPB for this entry; estimated at 3.8 g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for beef burger patty (NDB 21097) proportioned to the HPB total fat of 10.18 g/100g, with the bun component reducing the saturated fraction. This estimation is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for hamburger bun / beef burger composite
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for 'Beef Burger With Black Pepper' as a composite Singaporean dish. GI estimated at 66 (medium) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white-bread-based hamburger bun (GI ~70–72) weighted against the beef patty component (GI ~0, negligible carbohydrate contribution) and black pepper sauce (minor carbohydrate). Composite GI calculated proportionally: bun contributes ~85% of carbohydrate load at GI 70, sauce and garnish contribute ~15% at GI ~30, yielding a weighted composite GI of approximately 66. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (66 × 29.57 × 0.18 × 0.87) / 100 ≈ 11.4 g per 180 g serving. Estimation basis documented per Project Veritas v2 GI policy.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 487 mg/100g with low potassium offset (197 mg/100g) and insufficient dietary fibre (1.8 g/100g) to qualify for borderline sodium rescue
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish; if consumed, request reduced black pepper sauce, no added salt, and avoid additional high-sodium condiments such as cheese or processed sauces, as a standard 180 g serving delivers approximately 877 mg sodium — nearly 40% of the 2300 mg daily limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined carbohydrate 29.57 g/100g from white bun with low dietary fibre (1.8 g/100g) and sugar 5.95 g/100g, producing a medium glycaemic index of approximately 66 and a glycaemic load of ~11.4 per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should request a wholegrain or lettuce-wrap alternative to reduce the refined carbohydrate and glycaemic load; avoid sugary condiments, limit to one burger, and pair with a non-starchy vegetable side rather than fries or sweetened beverages.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat estimated at 3.8 g/100g from beef patty, trans fat estimated at 0.1 g/100g, cholesterol 29 mg/100g, and sodium 487 mg/100g collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of this dish due to its saturated fat, trans fat, and sodium content; if eaten, choose a smaller portion, avoid cheese and processed condiments, and balance with a high-fibre, low-sodium meal for the remainder of the day.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef is high in arachidonic acid (omega-6 fatty acid) which amplifies inflammatory pathways; trans fat estimated at 0.1 g/100g is pro-inflammatory; tomato is a nightshade vegetable that may exacerbate symptoms in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or minimise this dish; consider a plant-based or oily-fish patty alternative, omit tomato if nightshade-sensitive, and request no added black pepper sauce to reduce pro-inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef Burger With Black Pepper is a Singaporean Chinese-style fast-casual dish consisting of a grilled or pan-fried beef patty served in a white wheat bun, dressed with a savoury black pepper sauce and typically garnished with lettuce, tomato, and onion. The black pepper sauce — a hallmark of Singaporean Chinese cooking — is made from cracked black peppercorns, oyster sauce, soy sauce, and sometimes butter or margarine, giving the burger a bold, peppery, umami-rich flavour profile that distinguishes it from Western-style burgers. The bun is soft and slightly sweet, contrasting with the robust sauce. Per 100 g, the dish provides 263 kcal, 13.3 g protein, 10.18 g fat, and 29.57 g carbohydrate, with sodium at 487 mg — placing it in the borderline-high sodium category. Dietary fibre is low at 1.8 g/100g, reflecting the refined white bun. The dish has a medium glycaemic index (estimated ~66) and a glycaemic load of approximately 11.4 per standard 180 g serving. Clinically, the dish is unsuitable for hypertension due to high sodium and low potassium offset, requires portion control for Type 2 Diabetes due to refined carbohydrate and moderate glycaemic load, and poses moderate cardiovascular risk from saturated fat and trans fat in the beef patty. It is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to the pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid content of red meat and the presence of nightshade ingredients. The dish is commonly consumed at lunch or dinner in Singapore hawker centres, food courts, and fast-food outlets.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (record G00010085, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy beef burger data proportioned to HPB total fat — documented as adapted. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for white-bun hamburger composite — documented as analogous estimate. All mandatory multilingual names provided for Singaporean Chinese cuisine category.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (3.8 g/100g) is not separately reported in the HPB record for this entry; it is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 21097 (beef patty, cooked) proportioned to the HPB total fat of 10.18 g/100g, accounting for the bun component reducing the saturated fraction. This is an adapted estimate and is documented per source_match_notes. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite Singaporean dish is not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a matched composite entry as of 2026-04-16. 3. trans_fat_g set to 0.1 g/100g: estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for cooked ground beef patty; HPB does not separately report trans fat for this entry. 4. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish is not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry. 5. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient detail beyond the HPB-reported fields (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, cholesterol) is not available in the HPB Foodo database for this entry as of 2026-04-16. 6. GI of 66 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database white-bun hamburger composite; no direct GI measurement for this specific Singaporean black pepper beef burger exists in published literature. 7. Halal status is not confirmed; the dish contains beef and the HPB record does not carry a halal certification flag — is_halal set to false pending confirmation. 8. The food_group 'Grains and staples' and food_subgroup 'Bread-based dishes' are preserved from the HPB source record as the bun is the dominant carbohydrate vehicle.
